parsed.summary: The v0.4.0→v0.4.6 cumulative diff adds 6600+ lines across 84 files, introducing new retry/backoff machinery, BigModel error dispatch, and deleting process.mjs. Three material risks warrant blocking: (1) a deleted module that may still be imported at runtime, (2) retry logic on potentially non-idempotent API calls, and (3) concurrent state-file mutation across multiple modified modules with no apparent locking. These are exactly the class of failures that pass happy-path testing and surface under load or partial failure.

findings.title: process.mjs deleted — latent import breakage
findings.body: scripts/lib/process.mjs (67 lines) was deleted in this diff. If any surviving module statically or dynamically imports from 'scripts/lib/process.mjs' or './process.mjs', the import will throw MODULE_NOT_FOUND at runtime — but only when that specific code path executes. The diff shows glm-companion.mjs, glm-client.mjs, render.mjs, job-control.mjs, and session-lifecycle-hook.mjs were all modified in the same range, making it plausible that a re-export or conditional import was missed during the refactor. This is a silent bomb: it passes unit tests if the deleted path isn't exercised, and only detonates in production under the right invocation.
findings.recommendation: Grep the entire codebase for 'process' import paths (both static import and dynamic import()). Verify that no surviving file references scripts/lib/process.mjs. Add a CI gate that fails if any import resolves to a nonexistent file — the existing check-imports.mjs script is the right place for this.

findings.title: New retry.mjs (203 lines) applied to side-effecting API calls without idempotency proof
findings.body: retry.mjs is a brand-new 203-line module implementing automatic retry/backoff, introduced alongside modifications to glm-client.mjs (the API client). The commit message confirms 'auto retry/backoff' for BigModel errors. If the retried operations are API calls that create resources, submit results, or mutate server-side state (e.g., job submission, review posting), retries will produce duplicate side effects. The companion test file (retry.test.mjs, 352 lines) may cover retry mechanics but likely does not verify server-side idempotency. Under network timeout or 5xx conditions — exactly when retries fire — the client may double-submit.
findings.recommendation: Audit every call site where retry.mjs is applied in glm-client.mjs. For each retried operation, confirm either: (a) the operation is read-only, (b) the API supports idempotency keys, or (c) the retry wrapper checks for a prior successful response before re-executing. Document which operations are safe to retry. If any are non-idempotent, either exclude them from retry or add a deduplication guard.

findings.title: Concurrent state mutation across state.mjs, tracked-jobs.mjs, job-control.mjs with no file locking
findings.body: state.mjs, tracked-jobs.mjs, and job-control.mjs were all modified in this diff and share a state directory. If two CLI invocations run concurrently (e.g., a review and a status check, or two reviews on different threads), they may read-modify-write the same state file simultaneously, causing lost updates or corrupt JSON. The fs.mjs module was also modified (44 lines changed), which is the likely path for file I/O, but the diff stat doesn't show any file-locking primitives (no flock, lockfile, or atomic-rename pattern visible in the changed files list).
findings.recommendation: Inspect fs.mjs for atomic write patterns (write-to-temp + rename). If absent, add advisory file locking (e.g., proper-lockfile or fsync+rename) for all state file mutations. Add a concurrency test that runs two CLI invocations simultaneously against the same state directory and verifies neither loses data.

findings.title: bigmodel-errors.mjs error catalog may silently swallow unknown error codes
findings.body: bigmodel-errors.mjs (168 lines new) classifies BigModel API errors for the retry/backoff dispatch. API providers regularly introduce new error codes. If this dispatch lacks a catch-all branch that propagates unrecognized errors upward (rather than classifying them as non-retryable and silently returning), new error types will be invisible to the caller — the operation fails with no diagnostic and no retry. This is the classic 'silent failure' pattern in error translation layers.
findings.recommendation: Verify that bigmodel-errors.mjs has a default/fallthrough branch that logs the unrecognized error code and re-throws or wraps it in a known error type. The test file bigmodel-errors.test.mjs (189 lines) should include a test case for a completely unknown error code to confirm it isn't silently dropped.

next_steps: Run `grep -r 'process\.mjs' scripts/` and `grep -r 'from.*process' scripts/` to confirm no surviving import references to the deleted module.
Audit all retry.mjs call sites in glm-client.mjs for idempotency; list every retried operation and classify read-only vs. side-effecting.
Read fs.mjs write operations and confirm atomic-rename or locking for state files; if absent, flag as data-integrity risk.
Inspect bigmodel-errors.mjs for a catch-all/unknown error handler and verify bigmodel-errors.test.mjs covers unrecognized codes.
Run the full test suite and verify check-imports.mjs passes against the current tree.
